original_text: Assuming there is no afterlife then life is ultimately meaningless and pointless because we die. Note: Please assume that there is no afterlife when responding to my post. I am not religious. ALSO: By" meaningless," I mean" meaningless" to the person who is doing the dying. To the person who is alive and who will then die, life is meaningless. Assuming there is no afterlife, life is ultimately meaningless and pointless because we die. Death is the great eraser. Living life is as irrational as reading a book that, after finished, is immediately erased from your memory. It is comparable to that, too. It makes no difference if I, a 23 year old, die now or live until I am 100 and die. Either way, after my brief spark of existence I become nothingness. Not only do I remember nothing; I AM nothing. To me, it is as if my life had never happened. I 'll put it another way. Imagine the time before you were born. Have you remembered it yet? Of course not. You did n't exist. This is the state that we return to after death -- a state of nothingness similar to the state we were in before birth. When I die, I return to this state after a brief period of consciousness, and I return to it permanently. I might as well have never lived. Helping others does n't matter. They will die too. And then their descendants will die, on and on and on, until eventually the universe is destroyed or our galaxy collapses in an inevitable heat-death. Even if 1,000,000 people were helped by me, assuming they all die, then my life is still pointless and meaningless. For I will be dead and wo n't know what I did, and once they die, they wo n't know either. Attaining fame does n't matter and does n't truly make me" immortal." I 'll be dead, and again, death is nothingness. Thus, I wo n't be around to enjoy my fame. Enjoying the moment is possible, but does n't make life have a point. For once I die, all memories and all point is gone. Your point is predicated on the assumption that something is pointless if it does n't last forever. I 'm having a hard time understanding how you came to this conclusion. Imagine reading a book that, once finished, is immediately erased from your memory. That is what life is like. Reading the book is ULTIMATELY pointless. Because it is erased from your memory. Life is pointless in a similar manner. I would n't consider it pointless if I derived pleasure from reading it at the time. I mean if you really believe that everything that does not last forever is pointless, why do you do anything? Why not sit in a corner and stare at a wall until you die? I would guess that it 's because you derive pleasure from certain things in life and that you pursue these things just like everyone else does. My fear is that pursuing those things is irrational, that since death is coming and will erase all experience regardless, I might as well go ahead and die. Especially if there is more pain or dull moments than pleasure in life, why not just die? My fear is that pursuing those things is irrational Why is it irrational to pursue something pleasurable that will not provide pleasure of an infinite duration? That seems like an arbitrary condition to apply to the value of pursuing pleasurable things.
reconstructed_text:
Here is the complete reconstructed text, including both explicit and implicit parts, with logical flow maintained:

---

**Original Explicit Text:**
1. Assuming there is no afterlife, then life is ultimately meaningless and pointless because we die.
2. Note: Please assume that there is no afterlife when responding to my post. I am not religious.
3. ALSO: By "meaningless," I mean "meaningless" to the person who is doing the dying. To the person who is alive and who will then die, life is meaningless.
4. Assuming there is no afterlife, life is ultimately meaningless and pointless because we die.
5. Death is the great eraser.
6. Living life is as irrational as reading a book that, after finished, is immediately erased from your memory.
7. It is comparable to that, too.
8. It makes no difference if I, a 23-year-old, die now or live until I am 100 and die. Either way, after my brief spark of existence I become nothingness.
9. Not only do I remember nothing; I AM nothing.
10. To me, it is as if my life had never happened.
11. I'll put it another way. Imagine the time before you were born. Have you remembered it yet? Of course not. You didn't exist. This is the state that we return to after death—a state of nothingness similar to the state we were in before birth.
12. When I die, I return to this state after a brief period of consciousness, and I return to it permanently.
13. I might as well have never lived.
14. Helping others doesn't matter. They will die too. And then their descendants will die, on and on and on, until eventually the universe is destroyed or our galaxy collapses in an inevitable heat-death.
15. Even if 1,000,000 people were helped by me, assuming they all die, then my life is still pointless and meaningless. For I will be dead and won't know what I did, and once they die, they won't know either.
16. Attaining fame doesn't matter and doesn't truly make me "immortal." I'll be dead, and again, death is nothingness. Thus, I won't be around to enjoy my fame.
17. Enjoying the moment is possible, but doesn't make life have a point. For once I die, all memories and all point is gone.
18. Your point is predicated on the assumption that something is pointless if it doesn't last forever. I'm having a hard time understanding how you came to this conclusion.
19. Imagine reading a book that, once finished, is immediately erased from your memory. That is what life is like. Reading the book is ULTIMATELY pointless. Because it is erased from your memory. Life is pointless in a similar manner.
20. I wouldn't consider it pointless if I derived pleasure from reading it at the time.
21. I mean if you really believe that everything that does not last forever is pointless, why do you do anything? Why not sit in a corner and stare at a wall until you die?
22. I would guess that it's because you derive pleasure from certain things in life and that you pursue these things just like everyone else does.
23. My fear is that pursuing those things is irrational, that since death is coming and will erase all experience regardless, I might as well go ahead and die.
24. Especially if there is more pain or dull moments than pleasure in life, why not just die?
25. My fear is that pursuing those things is irrational.
26. Why is it irrational to pursue something pleasurable that will not provide pleasure of an infinite duration? That seems like an arbitrary condition to apply to the value of pursuing pleasurable things.

---

**Reconstructed Implicit Premises and Conclusions:**
1. **Implicit Premise:** If there is no afterlife, then all experiences, achievements, and relationships are ultimately erased by death.
2. **Implicit Premise:** The value of an experience is determined by its permanence (i.e., if it is not eternal, it is meaningless).
3. **Implicit Premise:** If something does not persist beyond death, it cannot have lasting significance.
4. **Implicit Premise:** Pleasure derived from temporary experiences is not sufficient to give life meaning.
5. **Implicit Conclusion:** Since death erases all experiences, pursuing anything (even pleasurable things) is ultimately irrational.
6. **Implicit Premise:** If life has no lasting impact, then all actions are equivalent to doing nothing (e.g., sitting in a corner).
7. **Implicit Premise:** The only way to avoid the absurdity of life is to accept that temporary pleasures are meaningless.
8. **Implicit Conclusion:** If life is meaningless, then the rational response is to end it (suicide) rather than endure pointless suffering.

---

**Complete Reconstructed Argument:**
1. Assuming there is no afterlife, then life is ultimately meaningless and pointless because we die.
2. Note: Please assume that there is no afterlife when responding to my post. I am not religious.
3. ALSO: By "meaningless," I mean "meaningless" to the person who is doing the dying. To the person who is alive and who will then die, life is meaningless.
4. Assuming there is no afterlife, life is ultimately meaningless and pointless because we die.
5. Death is the great eraser.
6. Living life is as irrational as reading a book that, after finished, is immediately erased from your memory.
7. It is comparable to that, too.
8. It makes no difference if I, a 23-year-old, die now or live until I am 100 and die. Either way, after my brief spark of existence I become nothingness.
9. Not only do I remember nothing; I AM nothing.
10. To me, it is as if my life had never happened.
11. I'll put it another way. Imagine the time before you were born. Have you remembered it yet? Of course not. You didn't exist. This is the state that we return to after death—a state of nothingness similar to the state we were in before birth.
12. When I die, I return to this state after a brief period of consciousness, and I return to it permanently.
13. I might as well have never lived.
14. Helping others doesn't matter. They will die too. And then their descendants will die, on and on and on, until eventually the universe is destroyed or our galaxy collapses in an inevitable heat-death.
15. Even if 1,000,000 people were helped by me, assuming they all die, then my life is still pointless and meaningless. For I will be dead and won't know what I did, and once they die, they won't know either.
16. Attaining fame doesn't matter and doesn't truly make me "immortal." I'll be dead, and again, death is nothingness. Thus, I won't be around to enjoy my fame.
17. Enjoying the moment is possible, but doesn't make life have a point. For once I die, all memories and all point is gone.
18. Your point is predicated on the assumption that something is pointless if it doesn't last forever. I'm having a hard time understanding how you came to this conclusion.
19. Imagine reading a book that, once finished, is immediately erased from your memory. That is what life is like. Reading the book is ULTIMATELY pointless. Because it is erased from your memory. Life is pointless in a similar manner.
20. I wouldn't consider it pointless if I derived pleasure from reading it at the time.
21. I mean if you really believe that everything that does not last forever is pointless, why do you do anything? Why not sit in a corner and stare at a wall until you die?
22. I would guess that it's because you derive pleasure from certain things in life and that you pursue these things just like everyone else does.
23. My fear is that pursuing those things is irrational, that since death is coming and will erase all experience regardless, I might as well go ahead and die.
24. Especially if there is more pain or dull moments than pleasure in life, why not just die?
25. My fear is that pursuing those things is irrational.
26. Why is it irrational to pursue something pleasurable that will not provide pleasure of an infinite duration? That seems like an arbitrary condition to apply to the value of pursuing pleasurable things.
27. **Implicit Conclusion:** Since death erases all experiences, pursuing anything (even pleasurable things) is ultimately irrational.
28. **Implicit Conclusion:** If life is meaningless, then the rational response is to end it (suicide) rather than endure pointless suffering.

---

This reconstruction maintains the original argument's structure while explicitly stating the underlying assumptions and conclusions that support the author's position.

original_text: Patriotism is the belief that being born on one side of a line makes you better. American patriots have a general mentality against immigration. Saying these people should not be allowed to become part of the country, is the same as saying they are worse, because they were born on a different side of a line, and americans are better because of the side of the line they are born on. This is prominent in many ads and political champagnes, namely the slogan “Creating jobs for americans”. I understand why politics use this slogan, because they are trying to get americans to vote for them, but this slogan is also prominent in ads made by private corporations. As if creating jobs for americans is morally superior to creating jobs for people of other countries. The companies launching these ads may be trying to win in the american market, so they can sell more of their product but the fact that this can increase sales shows that many americans hold the being born on one side of a line belief. I am not blaming the politicians or corporations running ads running these slogans, they are merely trying to win votes or make money from this mentality but really it is the citizens that have the belief that they are better, because of the side of a line they are born on, that are at fault. Patriotism is really just this belief. I would define patriotism quite simply as supporting one 's country, but not *necessarily* disparaging others. Therefore I would say that the antithesis of patriotism is not having a global perspective, but is in fact selfishness. Someone who attempts to extract gain for themselves at harm to their overall country is acting unpatriotically, someone who assists another country that is in worse shape instead of assisting their own can still be a patriot, but also recognize significant need in other nations and decide to assist them as well. Whether you support immigration or not, so long as you are supporting what you believe is best for your nation, you are still acting patriotically, and even if you decide to support another nation you can still be acting patriotically as well so long as you support your nation too. This is true, but, I think, supporting the common good is also more important than supporting your country. Yes, but the two are often one the same, especially when you live in a country as large as the U.S. most acts which serve the common good generally support your country.

1. Patriotism is the belief that being born on one side of a line makes you better.

3. Saying these people should not be allowed to become part of the country, is the same as saying they are worse, because they were born on a different side of a line, and americans are better because of the side of the line they are born on.
4. This is prominent in many ads and political campaigns, namely the slogan “Creating jobs for americans”.
5. I understand why politics use this slogan, because they are trying to get americans to vote for them, but this slogan is also prominent in ads made by private corporations.
6. As if creating jobs for americans is morally superior to creating jobs for people of other countries.

8. I am not blaming the politicians or corporations running ads running these slogans, they are merely trying to win votes or make money from this mentality but really it is the citizens that have the belief that they are better, because of the side of a line they are born on, that are at fault.
9. Patriotism is really just this belief.
10. I would define patriotism quite simply as supporting one's country, but not *necessarily* disparaging others.
11. Therefore I would say that the antithesis of patriotism is not having a global perspective, but is in fact selfishness.
12. Someone who attempts to extract gain for themselves at harm to their overall country is acting unpatriotically, someone who assists another country that is in worse shape instead of assisting their own can still be a patriot, but also recognize significant need in other nations and decide to assist them as well.
13. Whether you support immigration or not, so long as you are supporting what you believe is best for your nation, you are still acting patriotically, and even if you decide to support another nation you can still be acting patriotically as well so long as you support your nation too.
14. This is true, but, I think, supporting the common good is also more important than supporting your country.
15. Yes, but the two are often one the same, especially when you live in a country as large as the U.S. most acts which serve the common good generally support your country.

**Reconstructed Implicit Premises/Conclusions:**
16. (Implicit Premise) Patriotism, as commonly understood, often implies superiority over others based on nationality.
17. (Implicit Conclusion) The belief that being born on one side of a line makes you better is a flawed and harmful ideology.
18. (Implicit Premise) Political and corporate slogans like “Creating jobs for americans” reinforce the idea that Americans are inherently superior.
19. (Implicit Conclusion) The widespread use of such slogans indicates that many Americans internalize this belief.
20. (Implicit Premise) Citizens, not just politicians or corporations, are ultimately responsible for perpetuating this belief.
21. (Implicit Conclusion) True patriotism should not involve disparaging others but can coexist with global perspective.
22. (Implicit Premise) Selfishness—prioritizing personal gain over the nation’s well-being—is the opposite of patriotism.
23. (Implicit Conclusion) Supporting other nations in need does not necessarily make one less patriotic, as long as one also supports their own country.
24. (Implicit Premise) The common good and national interest often align, especially in large countries like the U.S.
25. (Implicit Conclusion) While patriotism is valuable, the common good should ultimately take precedence.

**Complete Reconstructed Text:**
Patriotism is the belief that being born on one side of a line makes you better. (1) American patriots have a general mentality against immigration. (2) Saying these people should not be allowed to become part of the country, is the same as saying they are worse, because they were born on a different side of a line, and americans are better because of the side of the line they are born on. (3) This is prominent in many ads and political campaigns, namely the slogan “Creating jobs for americans”. (4) I understand why politics use this slogan, because they are trying to get americans to vote for them, but this slogan is also prominent in ads made by private corporations. (5) As if creating jobs for americans is morally superior to creating jobs for people of other countries. (6) The companies launching these ads may be trying to win in the american market, so they can sell more of their product but the fact that this can increase sales shows that many americans hold the being born on one side of a line belief. (7) I am not blaming the politicians or corporations running ads running these slogans, they are merely trying to win votes or make money from this mentality but really it is the citizens that have the belief that they are better, because of the side of a line they are born on, that are at fault. (8) Patriotism is really just this belief. (9)

However, I would define patriotism quite simply as supporting one's country, but not *necessarily* disparaging others. (10) Therefore I would say that the antithesis of patriotism is not having a global perspective, but is in fact selfishness. (11) Someone who attempts to extract gain for themselves at harm to their overall country is acting unpatriotically, someone who assists another country that is in worse shape instead of assisting their own can still be a patriot, but also recognize significant need in other nations and decide to assist them as well. (12) Whether you support immigration or not, so long as you are supporting what you believe is best for your nation, you are still acting patriotically, and even if you decide to support another nation you can still be acting patriotically as well so long as you support your nation too. (13) This is true, but, I think, supporting the common good is also more important than supporting your country. (14) Yes, but the two are often one the same, especially when you live in a country as large as the U.S. most acts which serve the common good generally support your country. (15)

**Implicit Additions:**
16. Patriotism, as commonly understood, often implies superiority over others based on nationality.
17. The belief that being born on one side of a line makes you better is a flawed and harmful ideology.
18. Political and corporate slogans like “Creating jobs for americans” reinforce the idea that Americans are inherently superior.
19. The widespread use of such slogans indicates that many Americans internalize this belief.
20. Citizens, not just politicians or corporations, are ultimately responsible for perpetuating this belief.
21. True patriotism should not involve disparaging others but can coexist with a global perspective.
22. Selfishness—prioritizing personal gain over the nation’s well-being—is the opposite of patriotism.
23. Supporting other nations in need does not necessarily make one less patriotic, as long as one also supports their own country.
24. The common good and national interest often align, especially in large countries like the U.S.
25. While patriotism is valuable, the common good should ultimately take precedence.

---

This reconstruction ensures that all necessary premises and conclusions are explicitly stated, maintaining the logical flow of the argument.

original_text: If a child struggles to achieve in grade school it is virtually always the parents' fault. For the sake of this thread, we 'll exclude children with medically diagnosed learning disabilities; the knowledge and resource base for teaching said children is probably proportionate to the percentage they make up of the overall population. With a healthy child, income, neighborhood, peers and teachers should be filtered through a responsible parent ( s). A child who struggles in earnest or intentionally slacks off is a direct reflection of parenting. I am curious regarding potential for more public accountability held to parents who fail to direct their child. maybe a monetary incentive derived from public tax coffers paid to parents of children who maintain a passing grade, to be withheld if child dips below. I think parents are virtually 100 % responsible for producing scholastically successful children. One of the largest predictors for success appears to be not being poor. How would you account for that? It seems like fining poor people has some potential negative effects ... on the children. It would n't be a fine, since it would only be a suspension of" assistance" anyway. And poverty is a formidable obstacle but many, many people come from poverty to be wildly successful. That can not be a blanket excuse for poor academic performance. I believe the fulcrum of a child 's development has always been, and will always be the parent ( a living, mentally capable set of or just one parent). Yeah, but my point is that giving money to people that have successful children is going, ultimately, to lead to giving money to people that already have money, and withholding it from ones that do n't. Unless you make the reward high enough to overcome the *reasons* that poor parents are less involved ( And e.g. they do n't have time because they are having to work 2 jobs just to support the children), it 's not going to solve the problem. And that 's going to be very expensive. Furthermore, there 's a more fundamental *reason* that poor parents are poorer at helping their children ... they 're statistically dumber than richer people. Ignorance might be the cause, of course, rather than just lack of intrinsic intelligence. It would seem that a better approach would be to pay parents to attend training on how to be better parents, rather than rewarding the ones that have just happened to figure it out on their own. But honestly, I think you 're just being naive about actual statistical variation in native intelligence. There 's really no reason to believe that every person has exactly the same potential to succeed well in school. A simpler explanation is that native intelligence is probably normally distributed, like almost every other trait people possess. Native intelligence is the challenge. I wo n't delve into how this has infected my view, but I will say that it ties into why I think a moneyless society would over time revert to fiat currency. Some people can do some thing 's better than others ... thanks.

A child who struggles in earnest or intentionally slacks off is a direct reflection of parenting. I am curious regarding potential for more public accountability held to parents who fail to direct their child. Maybe a monetary incentive derived from public tax coffers paid to parents of children who maintain a passing grade, to be withheld if child dips below. It wouldn't be a fine, since it would only be a suspension of "assistance" anyway. And poverty is a formidable obstacle but many, many people come from poverty to be wildly successful. Unless you make the reward high enough to overcome the *reasons* that poor parents are less involved (e.g., they don't have time because they are having to work 2 jobs just to support the children), it's not going to solve the problem. And that's going to be very expensive. Furthermore, there's a more fundamental *reason* that poor parents are poorer at helping their children ... they're statistically dumber than richer people. It would seem that a better approach would be to pay parents to attend training on how to be better parents, rather than rewarding the ones that have just happened to figure it out on their own. But honestly, I think you're just being naive about actual statistical variation in native intelligence. Some people can do some things better than others ... thanks.

---

**Reconstructed Implicit Premises and Conclusions:**

1. **Implicit Premise (Supporting the claim that parents are responsible for academic success):**
   - *Parents have the primary influence over a child's environment, including access to resources, support systems, and motivation.*
   - *Effective parenting directly correlates with a child's academic performance, assuming no medical or cognitive disabilities.*

2. **Implicit Premise (Justifying the monetary incentive proposal):**
   - *Financial incentives can motivate parents to prioritize their child's education.*
   - *Withholding assistance (e.g., welfare benefits) from parents of underperforming children is a valid form of accountability.*

3. **Implicit Counterargument (Addressing poverty as a factor):**
   - *While poverty is a significant barrier, individual effort and parental responsibility can overcome it.*
   - *Success stories from poverty prove that systemic disadvantages are not insurmountable.*

4. **Implicit Premise (Defending the intelligence-based argument):**
   - *Native intelligence is a fixed trait that varies among individuals, influencing academic potential.*
   - *Societal structures (e.g., education systems) cannot fully compensate for innate differences in intelligence.*

5. **Implicit Conclusion (Final stance on the debate):**
   - *The most effective policy would focus on improving parental skills through training rather than financial rewards.*
   - *Acknowledging inherent differences in intelligence is necessary for realistic educational and social policies.*

---

**Complete Reconstructed Text (Original + Implicit Parts):**

If a child struggles to achieve in grade school, it is virtually always the parents' fault. For the sake of this thread, we'll exclude children with medically diagnosed learning disabilities; the knowledge and resource base for teaching said children is probably proportionate to the percentage they make up of the overall population. With a healthy child, income, neighborhood, peers, and teachers should be filtered through a responsible parent(s). A child who struggles in earnest or intentionally slacks off is a direct reflection of parenting. *Parents have the primary influence over a child's environment, including access to resources, support systems, and motivation. Effective parenting directly correlates with a child's academic performance, assuming no medical or cognitive disabilities.* I am curious regarding potential for more public accountability held to parents who fail to direct their child. Maybe a monetary incentive derived from public tax coffers paid to parents of children who maintain a passing grade, to be withheld if child dips below. *Financial incentives can motivate parents to prioritize their child's education. Withholding assistance (e.g., welfare benefits) from parents of underperforming children is a valid form of accountability.* I think parents are virtually 100% responsible for producing scholastically successful children. One of the largest predictors for success appears to be not being poor. How would you account for that? It seems like fining poor people has some potential negative effects ... on the children. It wouldn't be a fine, since it would only be a suspension of "assistance" anyway. And poverty is a formidable obstacle but many, many people come from poverty to be wildly successful. *While poverty is a significant barrier, individual effort and parental responsibility can overcome it. Success stories from poverty prove that systemic disadvantages are not insurmountable.* That cannot be a blanket excuse for poor academic performance. I believe the fulcrum of a child's development has always been, and will always be the parent (a living, mentally capable set of or just one parent). Yeah, but my point is that giving money to people that have successful children is going, ultimately, to lead to giving money to people that already have money, and withholding it from ones that don't. Unless you make the reward high enough to overcome the *reasons* that poor parents are less involved (e.g., they don't have time because they are having to work 2 jobs just to support the children), it's not going to solve the problem. And that's going to be very expensive. Furthermore, there's a more fundamental *reason* that poor parents are poorer at helping their children ... they're statistically dumber than richer people. *Native intelligence is a fixed trait that varies among individuals, influencing academic potential. Societal structures (e.g., education systems) cannot fully compensate for innate differences in intelligence.* Ignorance might be the cause, of course, rather than just lack of intrinsic intelligence. It would seem that a better approach would be to pay parents to attend training on how to be better parents, rather than rewarding the ones that have just happened to figure it out on their own. *The most effective policy would focus on improving parental skills through training rather than financial rewards.* But honestly, I think you're just being naive about actual statistical variation in native intelligence. There's really no reason to believe that every person has exactly the same potential to succeed well in school. A simpler explanation is that native intelligence is probably normally distributed, like almost every other trait people possess. Native intelligence is the challenge. I won't delve into how this has infected my view, but I will say that it ties into why I think a moneyless society would over time revert to fiat currency. Some people can do some things better than others ... thanks. *Acknowledging inherent differences in intelligence is necessary for realistic educational and social policies.*

---

This reconstruction ensures that all implicit assumptions and logical gaps are explicitly stated, making the argument more coherent and complete.

original_text: The highest form of relationship between men and women doomed to be romance. Maybe it has to do with being an adolescent and being new to dealing with the opposite sex, but I 'm beginning to fall under the assumption that the only purpose for guys to get to know girls is to eventually engage in romance." Why else do men engage women in conversation in public?" I wonder my perception might just be distorted after reading Neil Strauss 's *The Game* Why do I feel like chasing after girls and getting to know them when I have so many great guy friends already who I know care about me so much more Why ca n't I be friends with girls ( even pretty ones) and not feel insecure when I do n't try to advance some romantic agenda? Why do I find myself inevitably becoming attracted to them? I have so many more questions regarding the non-romantic side of male-female relationships but instead of writing an abhorrent block of text nobody could possibly be expected to read, I ask just the one title question thinking that the above questions are more like fragments of a bigger picture of misunderstanding I want to know what older, more mature people think. It also must distort my view to have gone to an all-boy 's school for the majority of my life. At times, I feel like the interplay between men and women is always ( at least always initially) a bs game where the man tries to show the woman how non-threatening and valuable he is from a non-romantic standpoint before she is comfortable with allowing herself to think of him as a safe partner. Something about that seems so sinister, cynical, and hollow to me that I ca n't stand it. Id just like to point out that this has never remotely seemed the case from my perspective as a lesbian. Beyond that, there are many people who want close relationships without romance, an obvious example would be asexual people. They can have close relationships that rival that of marriage, but how would you even quantify that? How can you even compare them? What about the relationship of a brother and sister? If you limit it to just heterosexual non-related men and women, then maybe you 're right, but then again who is to say what form of relationship is the highest, two very close lifetime friends who are platonic or a married couple? The bottom line is that people are different from you can relationships are hard to compare. Maybe for you, every relationship with a woman is motivated by romance. For someone other hetero guy it might not be. Sexual attraction is not something that just shuts off so even if you 're in a lifelong comitted closed relationship you 'll still probably feel attraction for other people. Thats just reality for most people, and its not necesarily harmful and its not really shallow. There are very few absolutes when it comes to interpersonal relationships. In short, I do n't need to feel guilty when I 'm attracted to people I know I ca n't have? Maybe I 'm too black-and-white to think that the murmurs of my id are natural and not evil. As for which relationship is *higher* from a quantifiable standpoint, I see what you mean. When I said highest, I meant it not from a perspective of quantity of some sort, but of a level of worth. Looking at that statement, however, I think you 're right again -- who is to put a romantic relationship above a friendship? It 's a bit extreme and morbid, but if I were to put someone 's spouse and someone 's absolute best friend in front of a train and gave that person one to save, whom would they save? Maybe I just find that once I reach a certain threshold with a guy-friend, I find that we become best friends, but when I reach that threshold with a girl-friend, I find that I want more. Maybe I have n't been around the block enough or had enough female friends to feel this way. I do n't have any lesbian friends ( as far as I know at least), so I do n't know how I would react to being great friends with one.
reconstructed_text:
Here is the complete reconstructed text, including both the original explicit sentences and the implicit premises or conclusions necessary for the argument to hold:

---

**Original Explicit Text:**
1. The highest form of relationship between men and women doomed to be romance.
2. Maybe it has to do with being an adolescent and being new to dealing with the opposite sex, but I'm beginning to fall under the assumption that the only purpose for guys to get to know girls is to eventually engage in romance.
3. "Why else do men engage women in conversation in public?" I wonder my perception might just be distorted after reading Neil Strauss's *The Game*.
4. Why do I feel like chasing after girls and getting to know them when I have so many great guy friends already who I know care about me so much more?
5. Why can't I be friends with girls (even pretty ones) and not feel insecure when I don't try to advance some romantic agenda?
6. Why do I find myself inevitably becoming attracted to them?
7. I have so many more questions regarding the non-romantic side of male-female relationships but instead of writing an abhorrent block of text nobody could possibly be expected to read, I ask just the one title question thinking that the above questions are more like fragments of a bigger picture of misunderstanding.
8. I want to know what older, more mature people think.
9. It also must distort my view to have gone to an all-boy's school for the majority of my life.
10. At times, I feel like the interplay between men and women is always (at least always initially) a BS game where the man tries to show the woman how non-threatening and valuable he is from a non-romantic standpoint before she is comfortable with allowing herself to think of him as a safe partner.
11. Something about that seems so sinister, cynical, and hollow to me that I can't stand it.
12. I'd just like to point out that this has never remotely seemed the case from my perspective as a lesbian.
13. Beyond that, there are many people who want close relationships without romance, an obvious example would be asexual people.
14. They can have close relationships that rival that of marriage, but how would you even quantify that?
15. How can you even compare them?
16. What about the relationship of a brother and sister?
17. If you limit it to just heterosexual non-related men and women, then maybe you're right, but then again who is to say what form of relationship is the highest, two very close lifetime friends who are platonic or a married couple?
18. The bottom line is that people are different from you can relationships are hard to compare.
19. Maybe for you, every relationship with a woman is motivated by romance. For someone other hetero guy it might not be.
20. Sexual attraction is not something that just shuts off so even if you're in a lifelong committed closed relationship you'll still probably feel attraction for other people.
21. That's just reality for most people, and it's not necessarily harmful and it's not really shallow.
22. There are very few absolutes when it comes to interpersonal relationships.
23. In short, I don't need to feel guilty when I'm attracted to people I know I can't have?
24. Maybe I'm too black-and-white to think that the murmurs of my id are natural and not evil.
25. As for which relationship is *higher* from a quantifiable standpoint, I see what you mean.
26. When I said highest, I meant it not from a perspective of quantity of some sort, but of a level of worth.
27. Looking at that statement, however, I think you're right again -- who is to put a romantic relationship above a friendship?
28. It's a bit extreme and morbid, but if I were to put someone's spouse and someone's absolute best friend in front of a train and gave that person one to save, whom would they save?
29. Maybe I just find that once I reach a certain threshold with a guy-friend, I find that we become best friends, but when I reach that threshold with a girl-friend, I find that I want more.
30. Maybe I haven't been around the block enough or had enough female friends to feel this way.
31. I don't have any lesbian friends (as far as I know at least), so I don't know how I would react to being great friends with one.

**Reconstructed Implicit Premises/Conclusions:**
1. **Premise:** Romantic relationships are inherently more valuable or meaningful than non-romantic ones.
   *(Implied by the statement that romance is the "highest form" of relationship.)*
2. **Premise:** Men's primary motivation for engaging with women is romantic interest.
   *(Implied by the assumption that the "only purpose" for guys to get to know girls is romance.)*
3. **Premise:** Socialization (e.g., attending an all-boys school) shapes one's perception of male-female relationships.
   *(Implied by the reflection on how the author's view might be distorted by their educational background.)*
4. **Premise:** Non-romantic male-female friendships are rare or difficult to maintain without romantic undertones.
   *(Implied by the struggle to be friends with women without feeling insecure or attracted.)*
5. **Premise:** Romantic attraction is an inevitable part of male-female interactions.
   *(Implied by the observation that the author "inevitably" becomes attracted to women.)*
6. **Premise:** Romantic relationships are the default or most natural form of deep connection between men and women.
   *(Implied by the comparison of male-female dynamics to a "game" where the goal is to become a "safe partner.")*
7. **Premise:** Asexual or non-romantic relationships are valid but harder to quantify in terms of "worth."
   *(Implied by the question of how to compare such relationships to romantic ones.)*
8. **Premise:** The value of relationships cannot be objectively measured or ranked.
   *(Implied by the statement that relationships are "hard to compare.")*
9. **Premise:** Sexual attraction is a natural and neutral aspect of human experience.
   *(Implied by the argument that feeling attraction is not necessarily harmful or shallow.)*
10. **Premise:** The author's personal experiences may not reflect broader truths about relationships.
    *(Implied by the acknowledgment that they may not have had enough female friends to form a different perspective.)*
11. **Conclusion:** Romantic relationships are not inherently superior to non-romantic ones.
    *(Implied by the challenge to the idea that romance is the "highest" form of relationship.)*
12. **Conclusion:** The author's views on male-female relationships are influenced by their limited exposure to diverse social dynamics.
    *(Implied by the reflection on their all-boys school background and lack of lesbian friends.)*
13. **Conclusion:** The "game" of male-female interactions is a social construct rather than a natural or necessary dynamic.
    *(Implied by the critique of the "sinister, cynical, and hollow" nature of such interactions.)*
14. **Conclusion:** The author's attraction to women is a natural response, not something to feel guilty about.
    *(Implied by the statement that they "don't need to feel guilty" when attracted to people they can't have.)*
15. **Conclusion:** The "highest" form of relationship depends on individual values and experiences, not objective criteria.
    *(Implied by the rejection of the idea that romantic relationships are inherently more valuable.)*

---

This reconstruction maintains the logical flow of the argument while explicitly stating the underlying assumptions and conclusions that support the author's reasoning.

original_text: Humans are going to die out because of an economic collapse. Bill Gade presents the argument better than I do so I will just quote him. There have been 4 jobs or activities which Man has engaged in throughout the centuries: Hunter / Gathering, Agriculture, Manufacturing, and Services. What 's next? What category can you imagine in which billions of people will be working in the future? It turns out that there is a final category. It is known as Unemployment. As Agriculture, Manufacturing, and Services become more efficient in the coming months, workers will have nowhere to go but to the unemployment line. At some point, Man 's artificial economic system will necessarily collapse. When that happens, the corporations which produce and deliver food will no longer have an incentive to do so. The cities will suffer great hunger and it will be the end of Man. The current jobless situation you read about in the news is not an ordinary part of the business cycle. It is the final stretch of the linear trend that began with the birth of our species. There is also a Youtube video to go with this opinion. I do realize that this is a very unhealthy idea to have whether or not that the idea is true or not, I just have not been able to beat it myself. I do disagree with Gade a little bit. I do n't think it would kill everybody, but the some few people that still know how to hunt and gather would probably survive as well people who were rich enough to have access to a lot of resources to start out with before the economy crashes. I probably believe this because I listen to Alex Jones too much. Something like this will take decades or centuries and wo n't be applied evenly across the entire planet. I do n't see how it 's possible society could n't adapt as the inevitability of a laborless civilization grows. Are you sure? Economic bubbles historically popped in a matter of months. I have trouble equating a financial bubble with the entire world economy. There are what, 5 thousand publicly traded US companies? Yes, if they all went belly up we would have a problem but someone would pick up the slack.

---

**Original Explicit Text:**

15. I probably believe this because I listen to Alex Jones too much.
16. Something like this will take decades or centuries and won't be applied evenly across the entire planet.

---

**Reconstructed Implicit Premises and Conclusions:**
1. **Implicit Premise (Supporting the economic collapse claim):**
   - *Economic efficiency in Agriculture, Manufacturing, and Services will lead to mass unemployment because automation and productivity gains reduce the need for human labor.*

2. **Implicit Premise (Linking unemployment to economic collapse):**
   - *Mass unemployment will destabilize the economic system because consumer demand (a key driver of economic activity) will collapse without wages.*

3. **Implicit Premise (Food production incentive loss):**
   - *Corporations rely on profit incentives to produce and distribute food, and without a functioning economy, these incentives will disappear.*

4. **Implicit Premise (Survival of a few):**
   - *Only those with pre-existing survival skills (e.g., hunting/gathering) or wealth (e.g., stockpiled resources) will endure the collapse.*

5. **Implicit Premise (Adaptation skepticism):**
   - *Society cannot adapt to a laborless civilization because economic systems are fundamentally dependent on human labor participation.*

6. **Implicit Counter-Premise (Challenging the collapse claim):**
   - *Economic bubbles are localized and temporary, whereas a global economic collapse would require a far more systemic failure.*

7. **Implicit Counter-Premise (Market resilience):**
   - *Even if major corporations fail, other entities (e.g., smaller businesses, governments) would step in to fill the gap.*

8. **Implicit Conclusion (Author's partial agreement with Gade):**
   - *While not everyone will die, the collapse will still cause widespread suffering and societal breakdown.*

9. **Implicit Conclusion (Author's uncertainty):**
   - *The argument is distressing, but the author cannot dismiss it entirely due to its plausibility.*

---

**Complete Reconstructed Argument:**
Humans are going to die out because of an economic collapse. Bill Gade presents the argument better than I do, so I will just quote him. There have been 4 jobs or activities which Man has engaged in throughout the centuries: Hunter/Gathering, Agriculture, Manufacturing, and Services. What's next? What category can you imagine in which billions of people will be working in the future? It turns out that there is a final category. It is known as Unemployment. As Agriculture, Manufacturing, and Services become more efficient in the coming months, workers will have nowhere to go but to the unemployment line. *Economic efficiency in these sectors will lead to mass unemployment because automation and productivity gains reduce the need for human labor.* At some point, Man's artificial economic system will necessarily collapse. *Mass unemployment will destabilize the economic system because consumer demand (a key driver of economic activity) will collapse without wages.* When that happens, the corporations which produce and deliver food will no longer have an incentive to do so. *Corporations rely on profit incentives to produce and distribute food, and without a functioning economy, these incentives will disappear.* The cities will suffer great hunger and it will be the end of Man. The current jobless situation you read about in the news is not an ordinary part of the business cycle. It is the final stretch of the linear trend that began with the birth of our species. There is also a YouTube video to go with this opinion. I do realize that this is a very unhealthy idea to have whether or not that the idea is true or not, I just have not been able to beat it myself. I do disagree with Gade a little bit. I don't think it would kill everybody, but the some few people that still know how to hunt and gather would probably survive as well people who were rich enough to have access to a lot of resources to start out with before the economy crashes. *Only those with pre-existing survival skills (e.g., hunting/gathering) or wealth (e.g., stockpiled resources) will endure the collapse.* I probably believe this because I listen to Alex Jones too much. Something like this will take decades or centuries and won't be applied evenly across the entire planet. I don't see how it's possible society couldn't adapt as the inevitability of a laborless civilization grows. *Society cannot adapt to a laborless civilization because economic systems are fundamentally dependent on human labor participation.* Are you sure? Economic bubbles historically popped in a matter of months. *Economic bubbles are localized and temporary, whereas a global economic collapse would require a far more systemic failure.* I have trouble equating a financial bubble with the entire world economy. There are what, 5 thousand publicly traded US companies? Yes, if they all went belly up we would have a problem but someone would pick up the slack. *Even if major corporations fail, other entities (e.g., smaller businesses, governments) would step in to fill the gap.* While not everyone will die, the collapse will still cause widespread suffering and societal breakdown. *This is the author's partial agreement with Gade.* The argument is distressing, but the author cannot dismiss it entirely due to its plausibility. *This reflects the author's uncertainty.*

---

</Explicit> <Explicit> What category can you imagine in which billions of people will be working in the future? </Explicit> <Explicit> It turns out that there is a final category. </Explicit> <Explicit> It is known as Unemployment. </Explicit> <Implicit> As Agriculture, Manufacturing, and Services become more efficient in the coming months, workers will have nowhere to go but to the unemployment line. </Implicit> <Explicit> At some point, Man 's artificial economic system will necessarily collapse. </Explicit> <Implicit> When that happens, the corporations which produce and deliver food will no longer have an incentive to do so. </Implicit> <Implicit> The cities will suffer great hunger and it will be the end of Man. </Implicit> <Explicit> The current jobless situation you read about in the news is not an ordinary part of the business cycle. </Explicit> <Explicit> It is the final stretch of the linear trend that began with the birth of our species. </Explicit> <Explicit> There is also a Youtube video to go with this opinion. </Explicit> <Explicit> I do realize that this is a very unhealthy idea to have whether or not that the idea is true or not, I just have not been able to beat it myself. </Explicit> <Explicit> I do disagree with Gade a little bit. </Explicit> <Explicit> I do n't think it would kill everybody, but the some few people that still know how to hunt and gather would probably survive as well people who were rich enough to have access to a lot of resources to start out with before the economy crashes. </Explicit> <Implicit> I probably believe this because I listen to Alex Jones too much. </Implicit> <Implicit> Something like this will take decades or centuries and wo n't be applied evenly across the entire planet. </Implicit> <Explicit> I do n't see how it 's possible society could n't adapt as the inevitability of a laborless civilization grows. </Explicit> <Explicit> Are you sure? </Explicit> <Implicit> Economic bubbles historically popped in a matter of months. </Implicit> <Implicit> I have trouble equating a financial bubble with the entire world economy. </Implicit> <Explicit> There are what, 5 thousand publicly traded US companies? </Explicit> <Explicit> Yes, if they all went belly up we would have a problem but someone would pick up the slack. </Explicit>
